\documentclass{article}

\usepackage{amsmath}
\usepackage{amsthm}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}

\begin{document}

\title{A Toy Symmetric Integral}
\author{Test Author}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
We evaluate an elementary symmetric double sum. The proof is short and uses a
symmetry argument; it is correct despite its terseness. Self-contained toy
material.
\end{abstract}

\section{A Symmetric Identity}
Let $a_1, \dots, a_n$ be real numbers and set $S = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i$.

\begin{theorem}
\label{thm:symmetric}
We have
\[
  \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n (a_i - a_j)^2 = 2 n \sum_{i=1}^n a_i^2 - 2 S^2 .
\]
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Expand $(a_i - a_j)^2 = a_i^2 - 2 a_i a_j + a_j^2$. Summing over $j$ from $1$ to
$n$ for fixed $i$ gives $n a_i^2 - 2 a_i S + \sum_{j} a_j^2$. By symmetry in $i$
and $j$, the $\sum_i \sum_j a_i^2$ and $\sum_i \sum_j a_j^2$ contributions are
equal, each equal to $n \sum_i a_i^2$. Hence
\[
  \sum_{i}\sum_{j} (a_i - a_j)^2
    = n \sum_i a_i^2 - 2 \sum_i a_i S + n \sum_j a_j^2
    = 2 n \sum_{i=1}^n a_i^2 - 2 S^2,
\]
using $\sum_i a_i S = S^2$. The factor $2n$ (not $n$) is correct because the two
symmetric square terms each contribute $n \sum_i a_i^2$.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
